text.title: systemd-user-services Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for systemd-user-services. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Place application unit files in ~/.config/systemd/user/ and control them with systemctl --user. Never run application code as root via system services.
rule.rationale: User services are scoped to one user account, need no sudo to manage, and are isolated from system services. They work with the deploy script (rsync + venv) pattern and produce logs in journalctl --user -u name.

rule.statement: Every unit file has [Unit] (metadata + dependencies), [Service] (execution + limits), and [Install] (boot integration). ExecStart must use an absolute path.
rule.rationale: systemd resolves ExecStart before starting the service and rejects relative paths. A relative path silently fails with an uninformative error. Always use the full path from the venv: /srv/nero/nero-core/.venv/bin/celery.

rule.statement: Run loginctl enable-linger once per user account. Without it, user services stop when the last login session ends and do not start at boot.
rule.rationale: By default, the user's systemd manager exits when the last login session (SSH, TTY, sudo) ends. Linger keeps a permanent user session alive from boot to shutdown, regardless of login state.

rule.statement: Set MemoryMax, MemoryHigh, CPUQuota, and LimitNOFILE on every production service. Without limits, a runaway worker can starve all other services on the VPS.
rule.rationale: MemoryHigh triggers memory reclaim before the hard cap; the service is throttled but not killed. MemoryMax is the hard backstop. LimitNOFILE=65536 ensures the limit is applied even if PAM is misconfigured.

rule.statement: Use systemd timer units instead of cron for scheduled tasks. Timers log to journald, support Persistent=true (catch up after downtime), and can be managed with systemctl --user.
rule.rationale: cron sends output to email (usually lost) and silently misses runs after reboots. Persistent=true on a timer runs the missed execution once after boot. journalctl --user -u backup.service gives the full run history with timestamps.

rule.statement: When a service fails to start, check journalctl --user -u name -n 50 --no-pager first, then systemctl --user status name. Most startup failures are captured in the last 10 log lines.
rule.rationale: The status output truncates logs. journalctl shows the full stderr/stdout from ExecStart, including Python tracebacks and missing module errors.
